input_candidates.truth_value: The solidification mode follows FA: L → L+δ → L+δ+γ → δ+γ. Ferrite nucleates first at grain centers.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 11 examines 3Cr strips annealed at 550°C for 1 h, 5 h and 9 h: plasticity increases gradually with annealing time, while yield strength does not change significantly after 1 h; TEM shows dislocation density gradually decreases with increasing annealing time; κ-carbides mainly precipitate in the first hour while recovery needs longer time.

input_candidates.truth_value: Dislocation density in Cr-containing as-cast strips is higher due to reduced κ-carbides which hinder dislocation formation under thermal stress, combined with increased solid solubility of C from Cr addition. In 9Cr, Cr23C6 precipitation reduces dislocation formation.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Slip band statistics after tensile fracture: as-cast 0Cr average spacing ~90 nm and width 13.4 nm; as-cast 6Cr average spacing ~80 nm and width 12.5 nm; annealed 0Cr average spacing ~190 nm and width 20.5 nm; annealed 6Cr average spacing ~60 nm and width 7.9 nm. Finer slip bands and smaller spacing correspond to higher ductility (dynamic slip band refinement, DSBR), but high initial dislocation density in as-cast 6Cr interferes with this relationship.

input_candidates.truth_value: The glide plane softening effect of nano κ-carbides leads to lower strain hardening rate in 0Cr. The disappearance of κ and increased dislocations in Cr-containing strips increase strain hardening rate.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Austenite lattice constants measured by XRD/JADE: 0Cr 3.70 Å, 3Cr 3.56 Å, 6Cr 3.56 Å, 9Cr 3.55 Å.

input_candidates.truth_value: After annealing, the unusual plasticity behavior (0Cr TE decreases, Cr-containing TE increases except 9Cr) is explained by the balance between κ precipitation (increases YS, reduces plasticity) and dislocation recovery (increases plasticity). In 6Cr, limited κ precipitation and dislocation recovery result in the highest annealed elongation.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 3(a) engineering stress-strain curves and Fig. 3(b) true stress-strain and strain hardening rate curves for as-cast strips are referenced but exact curve coordinates beyond tabulated values are not fully extractable from text.

input_candidates.truth_value: The average widths of slip bands: as-cast 0Cr 13.4 nm, as-cast 6Cr 12.5 nm, annealed 0Cr 20.5 nm, annealed 6Cr 7.9 nm. Finer slip bands with smaller spacing after tensile fracture associated with high ductility (dynamic slip band refinement), but initial dislocation density also strongly influences elongation.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 8(a) and 8(b) provide engineering and true stress-strain curves for annealed strips; strain hardening rate of all annealed strips except 9Cr clearly decreases compared with as-cast, with 3Cr showing the largest reduction about 500 MPa.

input_candidates.truth_value: For 3Cr steel, annealing at 550 °C for 1 h mainly precipitates κ and increases YS; longer time (9 h) reduces dislocation density and improves plasticity but risks coarsening κ. Optimal annealing time needs further optimization to balance coarsening and recovery.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Cr addition improves corrosion resistance of steel strips, as evidenced by grain boundaries being less visible in Cr-containing strips under OM after annealing, consistent with literature reports by Tsay and Tuan.
